typicalCauses: Worn spark plugs or ignition coils
diyChecks: Scan for additional cylinder-specific misfire codes (P0301–P0312)
Inspect spark plug condition and coil boots for carbon tracking
Check for vacuum leaks with engine running (hissing, unmetered air)
Verify fuel trim readings with a scan tool (short/long term fuel trim)
safetyNotes: Do not touch ignition components while engine is running
Allow engine to cool before removing spark plugs

typicalCauses: Worn or fouled spark plug on cylinder 1
diyChecks: Swap cylinder 1 coil/plug with another cylinder; recheck if misfire follows
Inspect plug electrode gap and fouling
Check coil connector and wiring for corrosion
Use a scan tool to monitor misfire counts while idling
safetyNotes: Disconnect battery before unplugging coils if manufacturer recommends
Engine and exhaust components are hot after driving

typicalCauses: Exhaust leak upstream of O2 sensors
Engine running rich or misfiring (root cause)
diyChecks: Verify no exhaust leaks between engine and catalytic converter
Check upstream/downstream O2 sensor voltage patterns with scan tool
Address any misfire or fuel trim codes first
Inspect for blue/white smoke indicating oil or coolant consumption
safetyNotes: Catalytic converter and exhaust pipes reach extreme temperatures
Do not work on exhaust until fully cooled

typicalCauses: Weak fuel pump or clogged filter
diyChecks: Inspect all vacuum hoses and intake boots for cracks
Clean MAF sensor with MAF-specific cleaner only
Check fuel pressure if gauge available (compare to spec)
Listen for vacuum leaks at idle
safetyNotes: Use only MAF cleaner on MAF sensors — carb cleaner damages the sensor
Fuel system is pressurized — do not open fuel lines without proper procedure

diyChecks: Check for black sooty spark plugs indicating rich running
Inspect EVAP purge valve operation
Review short/long term fuel trim on scan tool
Look for fuel odor or wet injectors
safetyNotes: Rich conditions can damage catalytic converter — avoid extended driving
No open flame near fuel vapors

diyChecks: Tighten or replace gas cap; drive several cycles to clear
Inspect EVAP hoses near tank and charcoal canister
Check gas cap seal for cracks
Look for cracked plastic lines under vehicle
safetyNotes: EVAP system contains fuel vapors — work in ventilated area, no smoking

diyChecks: Verify coolant level when engine is cold
Monitor coolant temp with scan tool — does it reach operating temp?
Feel upper radiator hose temperature after warm-up (carefully)
Check for coolant leaks at hoses and water pump
safetyNotes: Never open radiator cap on a hot engine
Coolant is toxic — keep away from pets

diyChecks: Inspect gas cap and filler neck seal
Trace EVAP lines from tank to engine bay
Check if recent fuel system work left a hose disconnected
Replace gas cap if seal is worn
safetyNotes: Fuel vapors are flammable — no sparks or open flame

diyChecks: Inspect EGR valve operation with scan tool command if supported
Check for carbon buildup in EGR passages
Verify vacuum supply to EGR valve (if vacuum-operated)
Look for rough idle or ping under load
safetyNotes: EGR passages may contain hot exhaust gas residue — engine must be cool

P0507.description: Idle Control System RPM Higher Than Expected
diyChecks: Clean throttle body with throttle-body-safe cleaner
Inspect for vacuum leaks at intake manifold
Check PCV valve — shake and listen for rattle
Verify no aftermarket intake without proper calibration
safetyNotes: Keep hands clear of throttle plate when cleaning with key on
